events.title: Founding of Gojoseon
events.detail: According to Korean tradition recorded in the Samguk Yusa and Dongguk Tonggam, the god-king Dangun Wanggeom founded the kingdom of Gojoseon ('Ancient Joseon') on the slopes of Mount Baekdu. Gojoseon is regarded as the first Korean state and forms the basis of Korean national identity. Modern historians place the actual emergence of a recognizable proto-state in the Liao River and northern Korean Peninsula region between the 7th and 4th centuries BCE, though the founding myth anchors Korean civilization at 2333 BCE.

sources.name: Samguk Yusa (Memorabilia of the Three Kingdoms) by Iryeon, 1281
Korean History: Discovery of Its Characteristics, Academy of Korean Studies

events.confidenceReason: Founding date is legendary; archaeological evidence supports proto-state formation c. 4th-3rd century BCE

events.title: Gojoseon Flourishes as Regional Power
events.detail: By the 4th century BCE, Gojoseon had developed into a significant state. It maintained trade and diplomatic relations with the Chinese states of Yan and Qi. Bronze culture was highly advanced, producing the distinctive Liaoning-style bronze daggers unique to Korean culture. The state expanded its territory and controlled important overland and maritime trade routes connecting Manchuria to the Korean peninsula.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, c. 100 BCE
Early Korea: Reconsidering Early Korean History Through Archaeology, Harvard-Yenching Institute

events.title: Han China Destroys Gojoseon
events.detail: Emperor Wu of Han China launched a major two-pronged military campaign against Gojoseon in 109-108 BCE. After a year of resistance, the capital Wangomseong fell and King Ugeo was killed by his own officials. Han China established four commanderies in the former territory. Lelang commandery near modern Pyongyang endured for over 400 years, heavily influencing subsequent Korean states through Chinese culture, administrative models, and writing systems.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian), Han Annals
The Cambridge History of China, Volume 1

events.title: Samhan Confederacies Form in Southern Peninsula
events.detail: In the southern Korean peninsula, the Samhan ('Three Han') confederacies — Mahan (southwest), Jinhan (southeast), and Byeonhan (south-central) — emerged as significant political entities by the 1st century BCE. Each confederacy comprised dozens of chiefdoms. The Jinhan confederacy would evolve into the Silla kingdom; Byeonhan into the Gaya confederacy; and portions of Mahan into Baekje. These chiefdoms maintained active trade with China and Japan and developed sophisticated bronze and iron cultures.

sources.name: Samguk Sagi (History of the Three Kingdoms) by Kim Busik, 1145
Sanguo Zhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms), Chinese historical source, c. 289 CE

events.title: Kingdom of Silla Founded
events.detail: According to the Samguk Sagi, Silla was founded in 57 BCE by Bak Hyeokgeose in the Gyeongju basin of the southeastern Korean peninsula. Originally the smallest and most isolated of the Three Kingdoms, Silla maintained a unique bone rank system of aristocratic hierarchy and developed the Hwarang elite warrior corps that became central to its eventual military success. Silla would ultimately unify the Korean peninsula in 668 CE with Tang Chinese alliance.

sources.name: Samguk Sagi (History of the Three Kingdoms), Book 1, 1145

events.title: Kingdom of Goguryeo Founded
events.detail: Goguryeo was founded in 37 BCE by Jumong (Go Jumong) in the Yalu River region. Goguryeo became the largest of the Three Kingdoms, at its peak controlling not only the northern Korean peninsula but also much of Manchuria. The kingdom was a major military power that repeatedly repelled Chinese imperial invasions, most famously defeating a million-strong Sui dynasty army at the Battle of Salsu River in 612 CE. Goguryeo wall paintings found in royal tombs are UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

sources.name: Samguk Sagi, Book 13 — Goguryeo Annals
UNESCO — Complex of Koguryo Tombs World Heritage Site

events.title: Kingdom of Baekje Founded
events.detail: Baekje was traditionally founded in 18 BCE by Onjo, a son of Goguryeo's founder, in the southwestern Korean peninsula. Baekje developed into a sophisticated maritime kingdom with strong ties to Japan and China. It played a crucial role in transmitting Buddhism, Chinese writing, and court culture to Japan in the 4th-6th centuries CE, profoundly shaping Japanese civilization. The kingdom's ultimate capital at Sabi (modern Buyeo) was a major cultural center before Baekje's fall to Silla in 660 CE.

sources.name: Samguk Sagi, Books 23-28 — Baekje Annals
Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), 720 CE

events.title: Buddhism Introduced to Korea
events.detail: In 372 CE, the Chinese monk Sundo arrived at the Goguryeo court from the Former Qin dynasty, bringing Buddhist scriptures and images. King Sosurim officially adopted Buddhism as a state religion. Buddhism spread to Baekje in 384 CE and to Silla in 527 CE, transforming Korean civilization. It produced magnificent temple architecture, Buddhist sculpture, and literary traditions across East Asia. Korean monks became significant transmitters of Buddhism to Japan.

sources.name: Samguk Sagi, Goguryeo Annals — Year 372
Buddhism in Korea, Robert Buswell Jr., Princeton University Press

events.title: Battle of Salsu — Goguryeo Defeats Sui China
events.detail: In 612 CE, Sui Emperor Yang launched a massive invasion of Goguryeo with reportedly over 1.1 million soldiers. General Eulji Mundeok's brilliant strategy lured the Sui forces deep into Goguryeo territory, then destroyed them at the Salsu River (modern Cheongcheon River). The Chinese suffered catastrophic losses. Only 2,700 were said to survive of the 305,000-strong eastern expeditionary force. The defeat contributed to the Sui dynasty's collapse. Eulji Mundeok is one of Korea's greatest military heroes.

sources.name: Samguk Sagi, Goguryeo Annals — Year 612
Suishu (Book of Sui), Official Chinese Dynastic History

events.title: Silla Unifies the Korean Peninsula
events.detail: After allying with Tang dynasty China, Silla defeated Baekje (660 CE) and Goguryeo (668 CE), unifying the peninsula. Silla then fought a war of independence against Tang China (670-676 CE), successfully expelling Chinese forces and establishing the first unified Korean kingdom south of the Taedong River. The Unified Silla period (668-935 CE) was a golden age of Buddhist culture centered in the capital Gyeongju, renowned as one of Asia's great cities and home to extraordinary temples, royal tombs, and Buddhist art.

sources.name: Samguk Sagi, Silla Annals — Year 668
UNESCO — Gyeongju Historic Areas World Heritage Site

events.title: Wang Geon Founds Goryeo Dynasty
events.detail: Wang Geon (Taejo of Goryeo) founded the Goryeo dynasty in 918 CE after unifying the Later Three Kingdoms by 936 CE. He established his capital at Gaegyeong (modern Gaeseong, now in North Korea) and created a centralized state combining Buddhist and Confucian principles. The name 'Korea' derives from 'Goryeo.' Taejo welcomed surrendered Silla royalty and Later Baekje refugees, pursuing an inclusive unification policy. The dynasty endured 475 years until 1392.

sources.name: Goryeosa (History of Goryeo), official dynastic history compiled 1451
A History of Korea, Ki-baik Lee, Harvard University Press

events.title: Gwageo Civil Service Examination Established
events.detail: King Gwangjong established the gwageo (civil service examination) in 958 CE, modeled on the Chinese imperial system. This meritocratic institution allowed talented individuals to enter government service based on scholarship rather than birth, challenging hereditary aristocracy. The gwageo produced generations of scholar-officials who upheld Confucian governance ideals. The system endured in Korea until 1894 CE — nearly 940 years — shaping Korean society, education, and governance.

sources.name: Goryeosa, Official Biography of King Gwangjong

events.title: Mongol Invasions of Korea Begin (1231–1259)
events.detail: The Mongol Empire launched six major invasions of Goryeo between 1231 and 1259. The Goryeo court fled to Ganghwa Island (1232) and maintained resistance for nearly three decades. During the invasions, Goryeo craftsmen carved the Tripitaka Koreana — 81,258 wooden printing blocks of the entire Buddhist canon — as a spiritual act seeking divine protection. The second set (completed 1251) survives at Haeinsa Temple and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Goryeo eventually submitted to Mongol suzerainty but maintained its dynasty.

sources.name: Goryeosa, Mongol Invasion Records
UNESCO — Haeinsa Temple World Heritage Site

events.title: Jikji Printed — World's Oldest Metal Movable Type Book
events.detail: The Jikji (Anthology of Great Buddhist Priests' Zen Teachings) was printed in 1377 at Heungdeok Temple in Cheongju, making it the world's oldest extant book printed with metal movable type — 78 years before Gutenberg's Bible. Metal movable type had been invented in Korea around the 1230s. The surviving volume now resides at the National Library of France. UNESCO registered it on the Memory of the World Register in 2001.

sources.name: UNESCO Memory of the World Register — Jikji (2001)
National Library of France — Korean Holdings Catalog

events.title: Goryeo Celadon Reaches Artistic Peak
events.detail: The 12th century saw Goryeo celadon pottery reach its artistic zenith, producing distinctive jade-green glazed ceramics considered among the finest ever created. The unique inlay (sanggam) technique allowed craftsmen to create intricate designs of cranes, clouds, and chrysanthemums. The celadon industry flourished near Gangjin in South Jeolla Province. Goryeo celadons were prized across East Asia and are now among Korea's most iconic cultural treasures, held in major museums worldwide.

sources.name: National Museum of Korea — Goryeo Celadon Collection
Arts of Korea, Metropolitan Museum of Art

events.title: Yi Seonggye Founds the Joseon Dynasty
events.detail: General Yi Seonggye (Taejo of Joseon) overthrew the Goryeo dynasty and founded Joseon in 1392, establishing one of the world's longest-running dynasties (1392-1897, 505 years). He adopted Neo-Confucianism as the state ideology, replacing Buddhism. The capital moved from Gaeseong to Hanseong (modern Seoul) in 1394, where Gyeongbokgung Palace was constructed. The Joseon dynasty shaped Korean identity through Confucian governance, the four-estate social hierarchy, and classical Korean culture.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty), UNESCO Memory of the World
Korea Old and New: A History, Eckert et al., Harvard University Press

events.title: King Sejong Creates the Hangul Alphabet
events.detail: King Sejong the Great promulgated the Hunminjeongeum ('Proper Sounds for the Instruction of the People') in 1443-1446, creating the Korean alphabet Hangul. Designed as a scientifically systematic phonetic writing system, Hangul's 24 letters (14 consonants, 10 vowels) were engineered to represent Korean phonology precisely, making literacy accessible beyond educated elites who used classical Chinese. UNESCO designated Hunminjeongeum on the Memory of the World Register in 1997. Hangul Day (October 9) is a South Korean national holiday.

sources.name: Hunminjeongeum (1446), UNESCO Memory of the World
King Sejong and the Creation of Hangul, Academy of Korean Studies

events.title: Imjin War — Japanese Invasion of Korea (1592–1598)
events.detail: Toyotomi Hideyoshi launched a massive Japanese invasion of Korea in 1592 with over 150,000 troops. Japanese forces rapidly overran the peninsula, capturing Seoul and Pyongyang within months. Korean Admiral Yi Sun-sin's brilliant naval victories using innovative 'turtle ships' (geobukseon) cut Japanese supply lines. Ming China intervened militarily. After six years of brutal warfare that devastated Korea's population and agriculture, the Japanese withdrew upon Hideyoshi's death in 1598. Yi Sun-sin died at the final Battle of Noryang in 1598.

sources.name: Nanjung Ilgi (War Diary of Admiral Yi Sun-sin), National Treasure No. 76
Samurai Invasion: Japan's Korean War 1592-98, Stephen Turnbull

events.title: Manchu Invasion Forces Joseon into Qing Tributary Status
events.detail: After the 1627 invasion and a second massive Qing invasion in December 1636, King Injo was forced to prostrate himself before the Qing emperor in a humiliating ceremony at Samjeondo near Seoul and Joseon became a Qing tributary. Princes were taken hostage. This subordination to the Qing — whom Joseon Confucian scholars viewed as 'barbarians' — created deep cultural scars and reinforced Korean nationalism and the concept of Korea as the true heir to Confucian civilization.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok — Injo Annals, Year 1636-1637
State, Society and the Joseon Dynasty, Martina Deuchler

events.title: Donghak Peasant Revolution
events.detail: The Donghak Peasant Revolution of 1894, led by Jeon Bong-jun under the Donghak religious movement, was a massive anti-government and anti-Japanese uprising demanding political reform, land redistribution, and an end to corruption. The Joseon government's request for Chinese military assistance triggered Japanese intervention and the First Sino-Japanese War (1894-95). Despite military defeat, the revolution forced the Gabo Reforms (1894-95) that abolished the feudal class system and modernized Joseon's institutions, though real power shifted to Japan.

sources.name: Donghak Peasant Revolution Records, Korean National Archives
Korea Old and New: A History, Eckert et al., Harvard University Press

events.title: Japanese Agents Murder Empress Myeongseong
events.detail: On October 8, 1895, Japanese agents led by acting minister Miura Goro attacked Gyeongbokgung Palace and murdered Empress Myeongseong (Queen Min), who had sought Russian support to counter Japanese influence. Her body was burned in the palace grounds. The assassination shocked the international community and galvanized Korean resistance to Japanese imperialism. It is regarded as one of the most brazen acts of state-sponsored assassination in modern history and remains a defining trauma in Korean national memory.

sources.name: Gojong Sillok, Official Joseon Imperial Records — 1895
Japan's Colonization of Korea: Discourse and Power, Alexis Dudden

events.title: Japan Makes Korea a Protectorate (Eulsa Treaty)
events.detail: After defeating Russia in the Russo-Japanese War, Japan forced Korea to sign the Eulsa Treaty (Second Japan-Korea Convention) in November 1905, taking control of Korea's foreign affairs and installing Ito Hirobumi as the first Resident-General. Emperor Gojong refused to sign and his seal was affixed by force. The five Korean ministers who signed became known as the 'Eulsa Five Traitors.' Korean patriot Ahn Jung-geun assassinated Ito Hirobumi in Harbin, Manchuria, on October 26, 1909 in protest.

sources.name: Eulsa Treaty (1905), Japanese National Archives
Japanese Imperialism 1894-1945, W.G. Beasley, Oxford University Press

events.title: Japan Formally Annexes Korea
events.detail: The Japan-Korea Annexation Treaty of August 22, 1910 (effective August 29) incorporated Korea into the Japanese Empire as 'Chosen.' The Government-General of Korea was established in Seoul. Korean language was progressively banned in schools; Koreans were pressured to adopt Japanese names under the Soshi-kaimei policy. Land surveys resulted in significant expropriation of peasant farmland. An estimated 700,000+ Koreans were conscripted as forced laborers during World War II. South Korea views the treaty as null and void from inception.

sources.name: Japan-Korea Annexation Treaty (1910), Japanese National Archives
Colonial Modernity in Korea, Gi-Wook Shin & Michael Robinson, Harvard University Asia Center

events.title: March 1st Movement — Mass Independence Uprising
events.detail: On March 1, 1919, thirty-three cultural and religious leaders publicly read the Korean Declaration of Independence in Tapgol Park, Seoul, triggering nationwide nonviolent protests. Over 2 million Koreans participated in more than 1,500 demonstrations across two months. Japanese authorities responded with brutal suppression — at least 7,509 killed, 15,961 injured, 46,948 arrested according to official Japanese figures (likely underestimates). The movement inspired the establishment of the Korean Provisional Government in Shanghai in April 1919. March 1 (Samil-jeol) is a South Korean national holiday.

sources.name: Korean Provisional Government — Declaration of Independence (1919), Korean National Archives
The Korean Independence Movement and US Policy, Frank Baldwin, Columbia University

events.title: Liberation from Japan — Korea Divided at 38th Parallel
events.detail: Japan's surrender on August 15, 1945 ended 35 years of colonial rule — celebrated as Gwangbokjeol (Liberation Day). Within weeks, the US and Soviet Union divided the peninsula at the 38th parallel as a temporary administrative arrangement. The 38th parallel boundary was decided in hours by US Army colonels Dean Rusk and Charles Bonesteel. This hasty division split communities, families, and infrastructure and set the stage for two rival Korean states and the catastrophic Korean War. No Korean was consulted in the division decision.

sources.name: US State Department — General Order No. 1 (1945), National Archives
Korea's Place in the Sun, Bruce Cumings, W.W. Norton

events.title: Two Korean States Proclaimed — Division Becomes Permanent
events.detail: On August 15, 1948, the Republic of Korea (ROK) was formally established with Syngman Rhee as first president, following UN-supervised elections in the south. On September 9, the DPRK was proclaimed in the north with Kim Il-sung as Premier. Both governments claimed sovereignty over the entire peninsula. UN General Assembly Resolution 195 recognized the ROK government as the only lawful government of Korea. The division formalized what had been a temporary administrative arrangement into competing sovereign states.

sources.name: UN General Assembly Resolution 195 (III), 1948
ROK Constitution (1948), Korean Constitutional Court Archives

events.title: Korean War Begins — North Korean Invasion (June 25)
events.detail: At 4:00 AM on June 25, 1950, the Korean People's Army launched a massive surprise invasion across the 38th parallel with 135,000 troops and 150 Soviet-built T-34 tanks. Seoul fell within three days. The UN Security Council — with the Soviet Union boycotting over Taiwan representation — authorized a UN military force. President Truman committed US forces the same day. Within weeks, UN/ROK forces were compressed into the Busan Perimeter in the far southeast, holding a defensive line against annihilation.

sources.name: UN Security Council Resolutions 82 and 83 (June 25-27, 1950)
The Korean War, Max Hastings, Simon & Schuster

events.title: Battle of Inchon — MacArthur's Amphibious Landing
events.detail: On September 15, 1950, UN Commander General Douglas MacArthur executed Operation Chromite — a daring amphibious landing at Inchon, the port city west of Seoul. Defying extreme tides and strong harbor defenses, the landing caught the KPA by surprise, severed their supply lines, and triggered rapid collapse of North Korean positions throughout South Korea. Seoul was recaptured by September 28. UN forces then advanced toward the Chinese border. The operation is studied in military academies worldwide as a masterpiece of strategic boldness.

sources.name: US Army Center of Military History — Korean War Records
The Forgotten War: America in Korea 1950-1953, Clay Blair, Anchor Books

events.title: China Enters the Korean War
events.detail: As UN forces advanced toward the Yalu River in October 1950, the Chinese People's Volunteer Army launched a massive covert intervention. By late November, 300,000+ Chinese troops overwhelmed UN forces, driving them back below the 38th parallel. The Battle of Chosin Reservoir (November-December 1950) became one of history's most brutal winter battles. China's entry transformed a near-victory into a grinding three-year conflict fought around the 38th parallel with enormous casualties on all sides.

sources.name: PLA Military History — Korean War Operations, PLA Academy of Military Sciences
The Korean War: A History, Bruce Cumings, Modern Library

events.title: Korean War Armistice Signed at Panmunjom
events.detail: After two years of grinding stalemate and negotiations, the Korean Armistice Agreement was signed on July 27, 1953 at Panmunjom. Signed by the UN Command (US), the KPA, and the CPV — but NOT South Korea — the armistice established a military demarcation line (roughly the 38th parallel), a 4km-wide Demilitarized Zone (DMZ), and prisoner exchange mechanisms. ROK President Rhee opposed the armistice, demanding full reunification. No peace treaty has ever been signed; the Korean War technically remains unresolved under an armistice.

sources.name: Korean Armistice Agreement (1953), UN Archives
The Korean War: A History, Bruce Cumings, Modern Library

events.title: April Revolution — Students Overthrow Syngman Rhee
events.detail: The fraudulent election of March 15, 1960 triggered massive student-led protests. On April 19, police opened fire, killing 186 and injuring thousands. University professors joined the marches. Faced with a mutinying military and general strike, President Rhee resigned on April 26 and fled to Hawaii. The April Revolution (4.19 Revolution) established the Second Republic. It remains a foundational event of Korean democratic tradition, annually commemorated as Commemoration Day for April 19th Revolution.

sources.name: ROK National Archives — April Revolution Official Records
The April Revolution of 1960, Journal of Korean Studies, Vol. 4

events.title: Park Chung-hee Military Coup
events.detail: On May 16, 1961, Major General Park Chung-hee led a military coup overthrowing the democratically elected Second Republic. Park cited corruption, social chaos, and communist infiltration. He ruled via military decree before winning presidential elections in 1963 and 1967. He signed the controversial 1965 normalization treaty with Japan (providing $800 million for economic development) and sent Korean troops to support the US in Vietnam. His 18-year authoritarian rule coincided with Korea's unprecedented economic transformation.

sources.name: Park Chung-hee Presidential Archives, ROK National Archives
Park Chung Hee and Modern Korea, Carter Eckert, Harvard University Press

events.title: 'Miracle on the Han River' — Economic Transformation
events.detail: From the mid-1960s through the 1980s, South Korea achieved one of history's most dramatic economic transformations. Under Park Chung-hee's state-led capitalism, GDP grew at an average 9-10% annually. The government directed credit to favored chaebols (Samsung, Hyundai, LG, Daewoo) and built heavy industries — shipbuilding, steel, chemicals, and electronics. Korea's per capita income rose from $82 in 1961 to over $10,000 by 1995. By 2000, South Korea was an OECD member, completing one of the fastest transitions from poverty to advanced economy ever recorded.

sources.name: World Bank — Korea Economic Development Case Study
The Developmental State: Development Theory and the Asian Development Experience, Meredith Woo-Cumings, Cornell

events.title: Gwangju Uprising — Pro-Democracy Revolt Violently Suppressed
events.detail: On May 18, 1980, following General Chun Doo-hwan's seizure of power and the arrest of opposition leaders including Kim Dae-jung, citizens of Gwangju rose in a pro-democracy uprising. Paratroopers deployed by Chun brutally beat and killed unarmed students and civilians. Citizens armed themselves and briefly seized the provincial government building. The military reoccupied Gwangju on May 27. Official death toll: 165 civilian, 23 military/police; estimates range up to 600. The massacre became the founding trauma of South Korean democracy.

sources.name: ROK Truth Commission on the May 18 Gwangju Incident — Final Report (1995)
Kwangju Diary: Beyond Death, Beyond the Darkness of the Age, Lee Jae-eui

events.title: June Democratic Uprising — Direct Elections Restored
events.detail: In June 1987, massive nationwide protests erupted after the Chun regime's '4.13 Declaration' refusing constitutional reform and after student leader Park Jong-chul died under police torture. Millions of office workers, housewives, and citizens joined students in 19 days of continuous demonstrations in 20 major cities. On June 29, ruling party leader Roh Tae-woo announced the '6.29 Declaration' accepting direct presidential elections, release of political prisoners, and press freedom. This inaugurated South Korea's Sixth Republic and modern democratic era.

sources.name: ROK National Assembly — Constitutional Amendment Records (1987)
The Politics of Democratization in Korea, Sunhyuk Kim, University of Pittsburgh Press

events.title: Seoul Summer Olympics — Korea's Global Coming-Out
events.detail: The 1988 Seoul Summer Olympics (September 17 – October 2) marked South Korea's dramatic entrance onto the world stage with 159 participating nations. Korea finished fourth in the medal table with 12 gold medals. The games showcased Korea's economic miracle and transformed international perceptions of a country most Westerners still associated only with war. Infrastructure investment transformed Seoul. The opening ceremony's Hodori mascot and the Arirang folk song introduced Korean culture globally.

sources.name: International Olympic Committee — 1988 Seoul Games Official Report
Seoul Olympic Organizing Committee — Official History

events.title: Both Koreas Join the United Nations
events.detail: On September 17, 1991, South Korea (ROK) and North Korea (DPRK) were simultaneously admitted to the United Nations as separate member states — UN Resolution 702 and 703. Both had previously been blocked for decades by superpower vetoes. The simultaneous admission was diplomatically significant: South Korea accepted DPRK membership as it pursued its Nordpolitik (Northern Policy) engaging communist nations. The same year, both Koreas signed the Agreement on Reconciliation, Non-Aggression, and Exchanges and Cooperation.

sources.name: UN General Assembly Resolutions 702 and 703 (September 17, 1991)
ROK Ministry of Foreign Affairs — UN Admission Records

events.title: Asian Financial Crisis — Korea's IMF Bailout
events.detail: The Asian Financial Crisis reached Korea in November 1997 when the won collapsed and foreign reserves approached zero. The IMF provided a $57 billion rescue package — the largest in IMF history at the time — with stringent conditions: corporate restructuring, financial sector reform, labor market flexibility, and trade liberalization. Korea's rapid recovery became a case study in crisis management. Major chaebols (Daewoo, Kia) went bankrupt or were restructured. Unemployment spiked from 2% to 7%. By 2001 Korea had repaid all IMF loans.

sources.name: IMF — Republic of Korea: Stand-By Arrangement (1997-2001)
Korea Development Institute — IMF Crisis Analysis, 2003

events.title: First Inter-Korean Summit — Kim Dae-jung meets Kim Jong-il
events.detail: On June 13-15, 2000, South Korean President Kim Dae-jung flew to Pyongyang for the first-ever inter-Korean summit, meeting DPRK leader Kim Jong-il. The June 15 Joint Declaration pledged reconciliation, economic cooperation, and reunification through a confederation. It launched the Sunshine Policy era: the Kaesong Industrial Complex was established, separated families had emotional reunions after 50 years, and cultural exchanges expanded. Kim Dae-jung was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in October 2000 partly for this achievement.

sources.name: ROK Ministry of Unification — June 15 Joint Declaration (2000)
Nobel Prize Committee — Kim Dae-jung Laureate Citation, 2000

events.title: ROKS Cheonan Sinking and Yeonpyeong Bombardment
events.detail: 2010 saw two major DPRK provocations. On March 26, the corvette ROKS Cheonan sank near Baengnyeong Island, killing 46 sailors; a multinational investigation concluded a North Korean torpedo struck the ship (DPRK denied involvement). On November 23, North Korea shelled Yeonpyeong Island with over 170 artillery shells, killing 2 marines and 2 civilians, wounding 19 — the first North Korean bombardment of civilian South Korean territory since the 1953 armistice. South Korea suspended inter-Korean trade and dramatically increased joint military exercises with the US.

sources.name: ROK Ministry of National Defense — Cheonan Incident Joint Investigation Report (2010)
UN Security Council Statement on Cheonan (S/PRST/2010/13)

events.title: Park Geun-hye Impeached and Removed from Office
events.detail: South Korea's first female president, Park Geun-hye, was removed from office on March 10, 2017 when the Constitutional Court unanimously upheld her National Assembly impeachment (234-56 vote, December 2016). The scandal involved her close friend Choi Soon-sil exercising extraordinary influence over state affairs without security clearance, coercing chaebols into 'donations,' and interfering in government appointments. Candlelight protests of up to 1.7 million participants sustained pressure for months. Park received a 22-year prison sentence, later pardoned. The impeachment demonstrated Korea's robust democratic institutions.

sources.name: Korean Constitutional Court — Decision 2016Hun-Na1, March 10, 2017
Yonhap News Agency — Park Geun-hye Impeachment Coverage

events.title: Parasite Wins Oscar — Korean Cultural Wave Reaches Peak
events.detail: Bong Joon-ho's Parasite won four Academy Awards in 2020 including Best Picture — the first non-English film to win. In the same period, BTS became the first Korean act to reach #1 on the US Billboard Hot 100 (2020). The 'Korean Wave' (Hallyu) encompassed K-pop, K-dramas (Squid Game, 2021), Korean film, K-beauty, and Korean food. K-content generated over $10 billion annually. Korea's global cultural influence had grown from near-zero in 1990 to a defining force in global entertainment, reshaping how the world perceived Korea.

sources.name: Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences — 92nd Academy Awards
Korea Creative Content Agency (KOCCA) — Hallyu Economic Impact Report 2022

events.title: President Yoon Declares Martial Law — Impeached and Removed
events.detail: On December 3, 2024 at 10:30 PM, President Yoon Suk Yeol declared martial law in a televised address, claiming the opposition-controlled National Assembly was engaged in 'anti-state activities.' Military units surrounded the National Assembly, but lawmakers voted 190-0 to lift martial law within six hours. Yoon rescinded martial law at 4:30 AM December 4. The National Assembly voted to impeach him on December 14 (204-85 votes). On April 4, 2025, the Constitutional Court upheld the impeachment 8-0, removing Yoon from the presidency — Korea's second presidential impeachment in eight years.

sources.name: Korean Constitutional Court — Yoon Suk Yeol Impeachment Decision (2025Hun-Na1), April 4, 2025
Reuters — South Korea Presidential Impeachment, April 4, 2025
